g.data-narration: Welcome to Direct Memory Access. This topic explains how computers efficiently transfer bulk data without CPU involvement.
Traditional programmed I/O requires CPU to continuously poll devices, reading one word at a time, wasting processor cycles.
g.text: CPU continuously polls I/O device, reads ONE word, stores to memory,
then REPEATS for every word. High CPU overhead!

g.data-narration: DMA solves this by using a specialized controller to transfer data directly between I/O and memory, freeing the CPU.
g.text: DMA allows I/O devices to transfer data directly to/from memory
without CPU involvement. CPU only sets up transfer, then continues working!

g.data-narration: The DMA Controller has three key registers: Address (source/dest), Count (bytes to transfer), and Control (transfer parameters).
The DMA transfer begins when the I/O device sends a DMA Request (DREQ) signal to the DMAC, requesting data transfer.
g.text: I/O device signals DMAC using DREQ line to request data transfer.
CPU programs DMAC registers: source/dest address, byte count, control settings.

g.data-narration: The DMAC sends a Bus Request (BR) to CPU. The CPU completes its cycle and acknowledges with Bus Grant (BG).
g.text: DMAC sends Bus Request (BR) to CPU. CPU finishes current cycle,
then sends Bus Grant (BG/HLDA) signal and releases bus control.

g.data-narration: With the bus, DMAC sends DMA Acknowledge (DACK) to I/O, places address on bus, and transfers data directly between I/O and memory.
g.text: DMAC takes control: sends DACK to I/O device, places memory address
on address bus, transfers data directly between I/O and memory.

g.data-narration: When count register reaches zero, DMAC releases bus and sends interrupt to CPU, signaling transfer completion.
g.text: When count register reaches zero, DMAC releases bus control, CPU regains access,
DMAC sends interrupt to CPU notifying transfer completion.

g.data-narration: DMA operates in three modes: Burst transfers entire block at once, Cycle Stealing transfers one word at a time, Transparent only transfers when CPU is idle.
Compare DMA to interrupt-driven I/O: DMA needs only one setup interrupt vs many per word, but requires additional hardware.
DMA offers increased CPU efficiency and faster transfers, but adds hardware complexity and potential cache coherency and security issues.
g.text: ↑ CPU Efficiency | ↑ Transfer Speed | ↓ Response Latency
↑ Hardware Cost | Cache Coherency | Security Risk (IOMMU helps)

g.data-narration: DMA is used in disk drives, network cards, graphics cards, and sound cards for high-speed bulk data transfers.
